title: Oxalic acid treatment for varroa in winter
subtitle: The single most effective treatment in the beekeeping calendar.
excerpt: A broodless winter colony is the only time varroa mites have nowhere to hide from treatment. A single oxalic acid application in December or January, when all the mites are on adult bees, kills over 90% of the varroa load. Miss the window and the mites come out of winter with a head start on the new season.
sourceNotes: NBU guidance on oxalic acid treatments, nationalbeeunit.com (2024). BBKA varroa management information sheet. Apivar and Api-Bioxal product datasheets for oxalic acid dihydrate application rates.

glossaryTerms.definition: Varroa destructor, an external parasitic mite that reproduces in sealed brood and feeds on adult bees. Present in almost all UK colonies; the primary cause of colony collapse if left unmanaged.
The window in winter, typically December to January in the UK, when the colony cluster has no sealed brood and all mites are phoretic (riding on adult bees). The ideal treatment window.

content.text: mites on adult bees with a single contact application. It has no effect on mites inside sealed brood cells, which is why the treatment only works during the
. Applied correctly at the right time of year, it is the most labour-efficient varroa treatment available to UK beekeepers.

The ideal treatment window is mid-December to late January, during a sustained cold spell when the queen has stopped laying. Check by lifting the crown board on a mild day (above 5°C) and looking for a tight cluster with no open brood patches visible. Do not treat in early December if the colony is still flying regularly on warm afternoons; there may still be brood.

In the UK, only approved veterinary medicines are legal for hive treatment. Api-Bioxal is the licensed oxalic acid product. It is available as a powder for solution (trickle method) or as approved vaporiser cartridges for sublimation. The trickle method is the most straightforward for most hobbyist beekeepers: dissolve the sachet in warm sugar syrup to the concentration printed on the package, then trickle 5 ml per occupied seam of bees between the frames.

Oxalic acid is corrosive. Wear protective gloves when preparing the solution and applying it. Do not treat more than once per broodless period; repeat applications increase colony toxicity without improving efficacy. Dispose of unused solution by diluting with a large volume of water before drain disposal; do not pour concentrate down a drain.

On a dry, still day with the temperature above 3°C, remove the roof and crown board. Count the occupied seams between frames. Draw up 5 ml per seam in a syringe and trickle it slowly along the top of each cluster seam, let the solution run down between the frames into the cluster rather than flooding the comb surface. Replace the crown board and roof quickly to retain warmth. The whole operation takes a few minutes once the solution is prepared.

in before treatment and count the daily drop over three days. Treat, then count again after 48 hours. A well-timed oxalic acid treatment produces a large drop of dead mites on the board in the first two days. Insert the board again in March when brood rearing restarts to set the baseline for the season's monitoring.
